topic: Te Pire mō Ō-Rākau, Te Pae o Maumahara/Ō-Rākau Remembrance Bill
description: Wednesday, 31 July 2024 | Māori Affairs Committee Te Pire mō Ō-Rākau, Te Pae o Maumahara/Ō-Rākau Remembrance Bill Hearing of evidence: Leah Bell Whitiaua Ropitini Linda Campbell
descriptionSummary: Te Pire mō Ō-Rākau, Te Pae o Maumahara/Ō-Rākau Remembrance Bill. Leah Bell. Whitiaua Ropitini

Um, you know, highlight key points. Uh, take your submission as read. So over to you, Leah. K. Thank you. Um, I'm Leah. I am from Watmore Caves. I was a petitioner, uh, as part of the Mara campaign to commemorate the New Zealand Wars as a public holiday at that time, but a statutory day of Remembrance.

Um, as I outlined in my submission, um, the visit to as a teenager for myself and my peers, was deeply influential in our decision to petition Parliament to have a statutory day of remembrance, and also to introduce, uh, education around the New Zealand wars into the curriculum.

So, firstly, I want to acknowledge that I completely toko, uh, the mana and their decision making and their thoughts that they have put forward to the Civic Committee. Um, and also want to raise that I stand firmly in my positionality as Tanga and Pakeha. So, what I would like to present to the Select committee today is a few things.

Firstly, that I'm aware there is a rebalancing of the curriculum taking place. Um, I have great concerns about this because I believe that this rebalancing, um, is a way to take out the very important nature of the curriculum, um, as it stands in which we understand history to be at its center, Maori history.

Um, and I am concerned about the ways in which there is an understanding that learning about these histories is somewhat burdensome on Al Tamari. I would like to stipulate that in fact, it is what is connecting for Tamari and for Pakeha children, Toi children, to have a connection to the ua, to have a connection to each other, and to understand our purpose and responsibilities here in, so there's a few different mediums in which I think Rako, uh, being returned to UA can be a place of maga can be a place of education and, uh, public engagement with the guidance

of ua, of course. Um, I think that pow children in particular being engaged in these histories is deeply valuable, um, because you learn a lot of different sensibilities that you would not get in mainstream education when you engage with these histories.

Um, one of those, you know, being orry, um, but also I think a deep sense of empathy is greatly missing right now in our, in, um, through Parliament, if I'm going to be honest. And through, uh, a lot of the dialogues, dog whistling and rhetoric that is being spread around at the moment.

Mm-Hmm. Um, and, you know, I'm of the belief that I wish that our parliamentarians had a mandatory, um, New Zealand history 1 0 1 paper that they had to take before entering Parliament, because I think it would inform policies. Um, and I just, I think, again, have that sense of empathy that, um, was generated when Mike could have visited, um, visited Orko and galvanized us to take action.

Uh, I think, again, the heaviness that they talk around these histories is, uh, is very prevalent and something that was, there was great concern around us as young people and our ability to, um, take on this heaviness, um, and speak, I guess, in a national context. Um, but I would also like to, you know, like to say that there are different mediums in which we can engage with these histories in a safe way.

And a few examples of that, uh, you know, the Rania album that was recently, um, recently released where a bunch of artists from across released their, um, music, um, writing about the events of Rania. Uh, and I've always said that arts is the best way to engage young people and engage, I guess, our country in a way, in a, in a medium that is accessible and invites curiosity rather than, um, debate, which I think is the key thing to bring up here.

'cause I don't think it's ne it's necessary for this history to be debated as such, but rather engaged with, with curiosity. Um, I also would like to, again, return back to the importance of young, young people engaging with these histories and the impact that it has. Um, recently I was a project co-lead on a called To May toy, which essentially means be brave, be creative, and it was getting 50 young people from around Waikato to engage through art mediums, which a lot of them do not have access to, um, and engage in, uh, discussions

and creative mediums around the topic of identity. And, and we were really lucky to invite the director of Kafa, Mato like Jonathan, to speak to all these young people. And we could see that the impact of, um, having a story that is, I guess, our history, um, the impact that having a story that is our history had on these young people, and which that they could see themselves as their own storytellers and have agency over their own lives, particularly in areas like, and where I'm from, a lot of young people, um, can be very pigeonhole into

their expectations for their life. Um, and I guess not be encouraged to broaden their horizons, be beyond their small town. And often what that means is, um, their own cultural values are devalued, I guess, in mainstream society and engaging with these histories, having rako be a place of national and international significance, as I outlined in my submission, is a way of reinforcing the manner of, um, our young people in particular and seeing, and I guess emphasizing to them that their stories, their place.

That all matters very, very deeply. Um, you know, I also want to acknowledge, uh, why this is deeply important to, you know, this process of repatriation and ideally, um, a reconciliation and apology process would be ideal as well. Um, is because, uh, this event, or Arco, or the New Zealand Wars is arguably the catalyst and the beginning of a great chasm of hurt in our nation.

And I want to acknowledge, um, the recent state abuse report that came through, um, and make the link there that, you know, we have Wau in this area, wau of mine, whose ancestors fled from Raco, and then they in turn fled from state care officials coming to take their babies and head along the same river on the Pune River that their ancestors once did.

And so this cycle is very, very linked. Um, and there's a really interesting, um, thought that was brought up by Vincent and O'Malley in some of his works where he said that Arra was talking to him, and he said that the hardship from KO is not the fact that he has to see the names of, um, the ary and, uh, around, um, as street names, but actually the fact that the Una are living it hard in many cases and have suffered, uh, continue to suffer under state care.

Um, so I want to bring, you know, I wanted to bring that up because I think we, it's so easy for us to see these events in isolation. Um, but it's not at all in isolation. It's very much linked to the everyday lives of Wana across, and particularly in wa where I'm from.

So I think that, um, I think, again, having a focus around Moana and also having a, a deep focus around the value of wahine and their stories is additionally really important. Um, and I know that, um, mat Toro and many other comar were want to continue to do that.

It was the stories of, uh, of Wahine in particular that inspired us. Um, because we often, you know, in a, in a Western context, don't get to hear the stories of, of women and children, um, in our, in a remembrance of wars and conflicts. Um, but I think, you know, the harm that was perpetuated at that time, um, and the abuse that Wahine in particular suffered at the hands of the, um, constabulary, um, and, and Imperial Soldiers is something that needs additional recognition.

And, um, also the, the placing, the recognition of Wahine and their agency as well, um, in this time as Warriors, um, and the roles that they played is something that I think also with this Bill has an opportunity to be additionally recognized. Um, I think, you know, I have talked about a lot of different areas in which this bill could be taken, but I think the main thing is that, you know, there is, there is endless hurt across our country.

And I think it's something that a lot of haha are not willing, excuse me, to reckon with, because it mean they think that it might mean we have to change our own understanding of our, um, of our own identity in many ways. But I think that if we can create places like to be a space of public engagement, discussion, storytelling, it is something that is liberating for all people across alt to speak truth to our history.

When we initially started petitioning, we would have a lot of, um, elderly phar actually approach us on the street and say, talk about the relief that they felt that they could talk about their own ancestors' actions, um, and talk about it in a way that everything was on the table.

Um, and I think that that's what we really need to do right now, is just put everything on the table and be really considered about our history, because I think there is an, A blind is on moment happening again, whether that be with the repeal of seven aa, whether that be with the Fast Track Bill, um, you know, my generation are feeling very anxious and very, very tired around the decisions that are being made currently in the government.

Um, uh, and I think this is a small action that recognizes the humanity of all peoples in Altura, but particularly, and those that fought there, um, and passed there. So I, um, I'm happy to answer any questions, but I guess my question is, um, how does the Select Committee and how is Parliament wanting to, um, encourage, I guess, uphold education within, um, within the space of, and, uh, how are you willing to make sure that, uh,

Sansa stories are, um, honored within the new curriculum here? If there's something, is that something that you have the capacity to answer at this time? Yes. Thank you.
